title: Fitting architrave around a door frame
subtitle: Mark the shadow line, mitre the corners, pin in place
excerpt: How to fit timber architrave around a door frame: setting the shadow line reveal, cutting the two mitre joints at the top corners, fitting and pinning the three pieces, and filling the mitre joints flush for decoration.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (c. 1905). Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Site Carpentry syllabus.

content.text: is a first joinery job: three pieces, two mitre joints, and a set of pin fixings. The critical skill is the mitre at the top two corners, where the two side legs meet the horizontal head piece. Architrave is sold in standard profiles (ogee, torus, pencil-round, modern flat); choose to match the existing profile in the room.
Mark a pencil line on the face of the door frame, 6 to 8 mm in from the outer edge, running along both side legs and across the head. This is the
. The inner edge of the architrave will sit on this line.

Cut the bottom of the left leg square at floor level (or 10 mm above the floor if skirting will continue past). Offer the piece against the left side of the frame with its inner edge on the shadow line. Mark where the top of the shadow line intersects the face of the architrave. This is the start of the mitre. Cut the 45-degree mitre at this mark with the mitre sloping away from the frame, so the top of the leg is the longer point.
Mirror the process for the right leg: the mitre slopes in the opposite direction. Test both legs against the frame before pinning either.

Lay a piece of architrave on the floor. Offer the left leg mitre to the left end and mark where the right leg mitre begins on the right end. Cut the right-hand mitre. The head piece, when held in position, should sit flat against the head of the frame with the shadow line at its lower edge and both mitre points meeting the leg mitre points cleanly.

Apply a small amount of wood glue to the back face of the leg. Pin it to the frame face with 40 mm oval nails, one near the top and one near the bottom, through the frame timber. Pin into the wall plaster through the back edge of the architrave at 600 mm centres. Punch the nail heads below the surface with a nail punch.

Apply wood glue to the mitre faces and press the head piece down into the mitre joints. Pin the head piece to the frame head with oval nails. Pin at 45 degrees through each mitre joint (skew-nailing) to pull the mitres tight.

Fill all nail holes with a fine cellulose stopper, sand flush when dry, and apply a coat of primer before gloss. The mitre joints should be filled with a flexible sealant-filler if there is any movement likely (new timber will dry and open the joint slightly over the first year). A caulk bead at the architrave-to-plaster back edge prevents any visible gap as the timber settles.

attrs.fix: Fill the joint with flexible decorator's caulk. Prime and paint over. The movement will be less in subsequent years as the timber stabilises.
attrs.cause: New timber drying out and shrinking after fitting.
attrs.symptom: Gap opens at the mitre joint after the first winter

attrs.fix: If the frame is out of square, adjust the two mitre angles so they add up to the actual corner angle, not 90 degrees. Measure the corner of the frame with a bevel gauge and transfer the two halves to the leg and head cuts.
attrs.cause: The door frame is not square, or the two mitre cuts are slightly off 45 degrees.
attrs.symptom: Mitre joints do not close cleanly
